html:
Roofing Specifications for Greer's Automotive and Intermodal Corridor

Greer sits at the center of two overlapping roof markets: the tier-one and tier-two supplier campuses that ring the BMW Manufacturing plant, and the warehouse product rising along the rail spur that feeds the SC Inland Port. Both markets ask for the same thing before they ask about price: documented specification packages.

Supplier-Park Roofing Standards Near the BMW Campus

Buildings supporting automotive supply chains around Greer are frequently held to insurance and quality standards that exceed a typical warehouse spec, even when the roof itself is a standard low-slope assembly. Facility and risk managers here routinely ask for FM Global-approved system components, documented wind uplift ratings, and a fastening pattern that matches the FM 1-90 or higher rating called out in their corporate insurance program, beyond what local code alone requires.

We prepare submittal packages that map the proposed TPO or PVC membrane, insulation, and fastener schedule against those FM approval numbers before a bid is finalized. On supplier-park buildings, procurement teams generally want that documentation attached to the proposal itself, not produced after award, since it often has to be reviewed by a corporate risk group before work is authorized.

Warehouse Construction Along the Inland Port Rail Spur

The rail connection running from the SC Inland Port out to the Port of Charleston has pulled a steady run of new distribution construction along the I-85 corridor near Greer. These are large-plate, low-slope roofs on tilt-up or pre-engineered steel structures, and most are specified with a 60-mil TPO or PVC membrane over polyisocyanurate insulation sized to current IECC thermal requirements.

Roof traffic near a rail-served facility tends to be heavier than a typical office park, with more rooftop mechanical additions and tenant fit-out penetrations added after the base building is complete. We flag that in our proposals: every future penetration through a membrane under an active manufacturer warranty needs to be flashed to the original system spec, or the warranty coverage on that section can be voided.

Warranty Documentation for Corporate and Institutional Owners

Corporate-held property in the Greer submarket is often owned by a group with a standing roof asset management program, which means the paperwork matters as much as the workmanship. We assemble No Dollar Limit warranty applications, manufacturer product data sheets, and as-built roof plans as a complete package rather than loose documents, since that is the format most asset managers request when a roof enters their portfolio system.

Where a general contractor or construction manager is running procurement on a new supplier building, we align our submittal schedule with their overall project schedule so the roofing section of the specification, typically Division 07, clears review before material is ordered.

What a Greer Roof Package Typically Includes

  • FM Global approval numbers for the specified membrane and insulation assembly
  • Wind uplift calculations referenced to ASCE 7 for the site's exposure category
  • Manufacturer NDL or system warranty application paperwork
  • As-built roof plans marked with drains, curbs, and rooftop equipment
  • Edge metal and coping details specified to ANSI/SPRI ES-1
  • A fastening schedule matched to the deck type and rated uplift pressure

Wind Exposure Near the GSP Airport Corridor

Greer's position along the open corridor near Greenville-Spartanburg International Airport places most large commercial roofs in a wind exposure category that pushes uplift pressures higher than a sheltered in-town site would see. We run that exposure category into the uplift math before recommending mechanically attached versus fully adhered membrane, since the wrong choice on a large-plate roof can mean a field failure at the perimeter long before the field membrane shows any wear.

Perimeter and corner zones get extra attention on these calculations. A roof that passes uplift in the field can still fail at the parapet if the edge metal and coping aren't detailed to the same standard, which is why we treat ANSI/SPRI ES-1 compliance as a line item on the proposal rather than an assumption.

Questions Owners and Facility Managers Ask

What insurance-related documentation do you provide for supplier-park roofs near BMW?

We provide the FM Global approval numbers for the specified membrane, insulation, and fastening assembly, along with wind uplift calculations and product data sheets. Corporate risk groups reviewing a Greer supplier facility typically want this attached to the proposal, and we build it that way from the start.

How do you handle roof penetrations added after a warehouse is occupied?

Every added penetration, whether it's a new curb, conduit run, or exhaust fan, has to be flashed to match the original manufacturer specification if the roof is under an active warranty. We document each addition and flag any that weren't installed to spec so the owner knows before a warranty claim exposes the gap.

Why does wind exposure near the airport corridor change the roof system choice?

Open exposure near GSP pushes uplift pressures higher than a sheltered site, which affects whether a membrane should be mechanically attached or fully adhered and how the perimeter and corner zones need to be fastened. We calculate exposure-specific uplift before recommending a system rather than defaulting to a standard spec.

Can you align your submittal schedule with a general contractor's project timeline?

Yes. On new supplier or warehouse construction we coordinate the roofing submittal, typically under Division 07 of the specification, with the GC or CM's overall procurement schedule so review and material ordering aren't holding up the project.

Do you work with corporate asset management programs that require standardized documentation?

We do. We assemble NDL warranty applications, product data sheets, and as-built roof plans as a single package formatted for portfolio-level asset management systems, rather than sending documents piecemeal as work is completed.
